cheatSheet:
# Node.js — Quick Reference

## Event Loop Phases
```
timers → pending callbacks → idle/prepare → poll → check → close callbacks
```
- timers: setTimeout, setInterval callbacks
- poll: I/O callbacks, blocks here if queue empty
- check: setImmediate callbacks
- process.nextTick() runs between every phase

## Core Modules
```js
const fs = require('fs')         // file system
const path = require('path')     // path utils
const http = require('http')     // HTTP server
const events = require('events') // EventEmitter
const stream = require('stream') // streams
const crypto = require('crypto') // hashing, encryption
const os = require('os')         // OS info
const cluster = require('cluster') // multi-process
```

## Express.js Quick Setup
```js
const express = require('express')
const app = express()
app.use(express.json())
app.get('/health', (req, res) => res.json({ status: 'ok' }))
app.listen(3000)
```

## JWT Auth Flow
```
POST /login → verify credentials → sign JWT → return token
GET /protected → extract Bearer token → verify JWT → attach user → next()
```

## Stream Pipe
```js
fs.createReadStream('in.txt')
  .pipe(zlib.createGzip())
  .pipe(fs.createWriteStream('out.txt.gz'))
```

## PM2 Commands
```bash
pm2 start app.js -i max   # cluster mode
pm2 status
pm2 logs
pm2 reload app            # zero-downtime reload
pm2 save && pm2 startup   # persist on reboot
```

resources.title: Node.js Official Documentation
resources.author: OpenJS Foundation
resources.justification: Primary reference for all built-in modules, event loop internals, and stream APIs. Essential reading for any Node.js interview.

resources.title: Node.js Design Patterns (3rd Edition)
resources.author: Mario Casciaro, Luciano Mammino
resources.justification: The definitive book on Node.js patterns — covers streams, async patterns, microservices, and production architecture in depth.

resources.title: The Node.js Event Loop, Timers, and process.nextTick()
resources.author: Node.js Foundation
resources.justification: The canonical explanation of the event loop phases — understanding this is required for senior Node.js interviews.

resources.title: Express.js Documentation
resources.author: OpenJS Foundation
resources.justification: Complete API reference for routing, middleware, error handling, and request/response objects.

levels.name: Novice
levels.description: Can explain what Node.js is, understands non-blocking I/O conceptually, and can build a simple HTTP server.
observableSkills: Explain what the event loop is and why Node.js is single-threaded
Create an HTTP server with the built-in http module
Read and write files using fs.readFile and fs.writeFile

milestoneProject.title: Hello Node Server
milestoneProject.description: Build an HTTP server that reads a JSON file from disk and returns its contents. Handle 404 for unknown routes.

levels.name: Advanced Beginner
levels.description: Comfortable with Express.js, middleware, and basic REST API design. Understands async/await and Promises.
observableSkills: Build a CRUD REST API with Express.js
Write middleware for logging, auth, and error handling
Use async/await with proper try/catch error handling

milestoneProject.title: CRUD API with Express
milestoneProject.description: Build a REST API for a to-do list with GET, POST, PUT, DELETE routes, input validation, and a global error handler.

levels.name: Competent
levels.description: Implements JWT authentication, database integration, file uploads, and understands streams and buffers.
observableSkills: Implement JWT-based auth with refresh tokens
Integrate Prisma or Mongoose for database access
Handle file uploads with Multer and pipe streams

milestoneProject.title: Authenticated File API
milestoneProject.description: Build an API with JWT auth, user-scoped file uploads stored on disk (Multer), and streaming file downloads.

levels.name: Proficient
levels.description: Designs production-grade APIs with security hardening, real-time WebSocket features, comprehensive testing, and Docker deployment.
observableSkills: Implement rate limiting, Helmet, and CORS correctly
Build a real-time feature with Socket.io
Write integration tests with Jest and Supertest
Dockerize a Node.js application with multi-stage builds

milestoneProject.title: Production-Ready Chat API
milestoneProject.description: Build a real-time chat API with Socket.io, JWT auth, rate limiting, Helmet, and full test coverage with Jest/Supertest. Deploy with Docker Compose.

levels.name: Expert
levels.description: Architects scalable Node.js systems with cluster mode, worker threads, advanced stream processing, and deep event loop knowledge.
observableSkills: Use cluster module and PM2 cluster mode for multi-core utilization
Implement worker threads for CPU-intensive tasks
Design resilient systems with circuit breakers and graceful shutdown
Profile and optimize Node.js memory leaks and CPU bottlenecks

milestoneProject.title: High-Throughput Data Pipeline
milestoneProject.description: Build a Node.js service that processes large CSV files using Transform streams, runs CPU-intensive parsing in worker threads, and serves results via a clustered HTTP API monitored with Prometheus.

plan.overview: Master Node.js for software engineering interviews — from event loop internals to production deployment.
plan.skippedTopics: Deno, Bun, and vendor-specific cloud SDKs are excluded as they are rarely asked in general Node.js interviews.

sessions.title: Node.js Fundamentals — Event Loop, Non-blocking I/O, Modules
objectives: Explain the event loop phases and the execution order of setTimeout, setImmediate, and process.nextTick
Distinguish CommonJS from ES Modules and know when to use each
Use EventEmitter to build an event-driven component
Convert callback-style code to Promises and async/await

activities.description: Write a script that logs the execution order of: synchronous code, process.nextTick, Promise.then, setImmediate, and setTimeout(0). Predict the output before running it, then verify.
Build a custom EventEmitter-based PubSub system that supports subscribe, publish, and unsubscribe. Test with multiple listeners on the same event.

reviewQuestions: What are the phases of the Node.js event loop and what runs in each phase?:::The event loop has 6 phases: (1) timers — runs setTimeout and setInterval callbacks whose delay has elapsed; (2) pending callbacks — I/O error callbacks deferred from the previous iteration; (3) idle/prepare — internal Node.js use; (4) poll — retrieves new I/O events and executes their callbacks; blocks here if the queue is empty and no timers are pending; (5) check — runs setImmediate callbacks; (6) close callbacks — e.g., socket.destroy() callbacks. Between each phase, Node.js drains the microtask queue: first process.nextTick callbacks, then Promise .then handlers.

What is the difference between process.nextTick() and setImmediate()?:::process.nextTick() queues a callback to run at the end of the current phase, before the event loop moves to the next phase — even before I/O callbacks. setImmediate() queues a callback to run in the 'check' phase, which comes after the poll phase. In practice, nextTick fires earlier than setImmediate. nextTick is useful for deferring work within the same iteration; setImmediate is useful for yielding to I/O before continuing work.

Why should you avoid blocking the event loop with synchronous operations?:::Node.js processes all JavaScript on a single thread. A blocking synchronous operation (e.g., fs.readFileSync on a large file, a tight CPU loop, JSON.parse on a 50 MB string) prevents the event loop from processing any other callbacks for its duration. During this time, all incoming HTTP requests, database responses, and timer callbacks are queued but not processed — the server becomes unresponsive. Non-blocking async I/O delegates waiting to the OS and returns immediately, keeping the thread free.

What is the difference between CommonJS require() and ES Module import?:::require() is synchronous — it reads, compiles, and executes the module immediately when called. Circular dependencies are partially resolved (the requiring module gets whatever has been exported so far). import is static — the module graph is resolved and all imports are bound before any code executes. This enables tree-shaking and top-level await. ESM modules are always in strict mode. In Node.js, use .mjs extension or set "type": "module" in package.json to use ESM.
sessions.successCriteria: Can draw the event loop phases, explain the execution order of nextTick/Promise/setImmediate/setTimeout, and write an EventEmitter-based class with proper error handling.

sessions.title: Express.js — Routing, Middleware, Error Handling, Templating
objectives: Structure an Express application with separate route files and middleware modules
Implement middleware for authentication, logging, and request validation
Write a global error handler that categorizes different error types
Use Express Router to organize routes by resource

activities.description: Build a complete Express app for a Blog API: routes for posts (CRUD), auth middleware that checks a Bearer token, a request logger middleware that measures response time, and a global error handler. No database needed — use an in-memory array.
Add request body validation middleware using Joi or zod. Test that invalid bodies return 400 with clear field-level error messages before the route handler is ever reached.

reviewQuestions: How does Express middleware work and what does calling next(err) do?:::Middleware functions are executed in the order they are registered with app.use() or app.METHOD(). Each middleware receives (req, res, next). Calling next() passes control to the next middleware in the stack. Calling next(err) skips all remaining regular middleware and jumps directly to the next error-handling middleware — a function with the signature (err, req, res, next). This is how Express implements centralized error handling: any route or middleware can call next(err) and the error flows to the global handler.

Why must the error handler be registered last in an Express application?:::Express processes middleware in registration order. If the error handler were registered before routes, Express would match the error handler as a regular middleware (since its signature has 4 params, Express recognizes it as an error handler only in error state). Registering it last ensures: all routes are tried first, route handlers can call next(err) to reach the handler, and no routes are accidentally intercepted by the error handler during normal request flow.

What is the difference between express.json() and express.urlencoded()?:::express.json() parses requests with Content-Type: application/json and populates req.body with a JavaScript object. It is used for REST APIs receiving JSON payloads. express.urlencoded() parses requests with Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded (the encoding used by HTML forms) and also populates req.body. The extended option controls whether to use the qs library (true, supports nested objects) or the querystring module (false, flat key-value). Both are built into Express 4.16+ — no separate body-parser package needed.

How do you handle async errors in Express route handlers?:::Express does not automatically catch errors thrown by async functions. You must either: (1) wrap the handler body in try/catch and call next(err) in the catch block — the standard pattern; (2) create a wrapper function that wraps any async handler and calls next(err) if the promise rejects; or (3) use Express 5 (async error handling is built in). Without this, an unhandled promise rejection in a route handler will cause the request to hang forever.
sessions.successCriteria: Can build a complete Express API with route separation, auth middleware, body validation, and a global error handler that correctly handles async errors from all routes.

sessions.title: REST API Design — CRUD, Validation, Pagination, Versioning
objectives: Design a RESTful URL structure for a given domain model
Implement pagination with cursor-based or offset-based strategies
Validate request bodies with zod or Joi and return structured error responses
Choose and implement an API versioning strategy

activities.description: Design the complete URL structure for an e-commerce API covering: users, products, orders, reviews, and categories. For each resource, specify the HTTP method, URL, request body, and response for all CRUD operations.
Implement a paginated list endpoint with both offset-based and cursor-based pagination. Measure the query performance difference on a table with 100,000 rows.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between offset-based and cursor-based pagination?:::Offset-based pagination uses LIMIT and OFFSET: page=3&limit=20 fetches rows 41-60. It is simple to implement but has two problems: (1) if rows are inserted or deleted between requests, pages shift and you get duplicates or missing items; (2) large OFFSET values are slow because the DB must scan and discard all preceding rows. Cursor-based pagination uses an opaque token (usually the last item's ID or timestamp): 'give me 20 items after cursor=abc123'. The DB uses a WHERE id > 'abc123' index seek — O(1) regardless of page depth. Cursor pagination is preferred for large datasets and real-time data.

Why should you validate API inputs and return structured errors?:::Unvalidated inputs are the source of most injection attacks (SQL injection, XSS, command injection). Beyond security, validation ensures your business logic only receives data that matches its expectations, preventing runtime crashes and data corruption. Structured error responses (per-field error messages, consistent format) allow API clients and frontend apps to display meaningful feedback to users rather than generic 'Something went wrong' messages.

What HTTP status code should a POST that creates a resource return, and what header should accompany it?:::201 Created, with a Location header pointing to the newly created resource's URL. For example: Location: /api/v1/products/abc123. The 201 status signals to the client that a new resource was created (not just 200 OK). Some APIs also return the created resource in the response body to save the client a round-trip GET request.

What are the trade-offs of URL versioning vs header versioning for REST APIs?:::URL versioning (/api/v1/) is explicit, easy to test in browsers and curl, cacheable by HTTP infrastructure, and immediately obvious to developers. The downside is that it violates strict REST principles (the version is not part of the resource identifier). Header versioning (via Accept or custom headers) is more REST-pure and keeps URLs clean, but is harder to test, not cacheable by default CDN rules, and invisible to casual inspection. For most teams, URL versioning is pragmatically superior.
sessions.successCriteria: Can design a complete REST API URL structure, implement paginated list endpoints with validation, and explain the trade-offs of different versioning strategies.

sessions.title: Authentication — JWT, Passport, bcrypt, OAuth2
objectives: Hash passwords with bcrypt and explain why bcrypt is preferred over SHA-256
Implement JWT access and refresh token rotation
Configure Passport.js with local and JWT strategies
Build an OAuth2 login flow with a third-party provider

activities.description: Build a complete auth system: POST /register (hash password, create user), POST /login (verify password, return JWT + refresh cookie), GET /me (require JWT), POST /refresh (rotate access token), POST /logout (invalidate refresh token in DB).
Set up Google OAuth2 with Passport.js. On first login, create the user. On subsequent logins, find the existing user by googleId. Return a JWT to the frontend via redirect.

reviewQuestions: Why is bcrypt preferred over SHA-256 or MD5 for password hashing?:::SHA-256 and MD5 are designed to be fast — they can hash billions of passwords per second on a GPU, making brute-force attacks trivial. bcrypt is deliberately slow: the cost factor (e.g., 12 rounds = 2^12 iterations) means each hash attempt takes ~100-300ms on a modern CPU, reducing GPU brute-force speed by orders of magnitude. bcrypt also automatically generates and embeds a random salt in the output, preventing rainbow table attacks. The cost factor is adjustable to stay ahead of hardware improvements.

What is the difference between an access token and a refresh token in JWT auth?:::An access token is a short-lived JWT (typically 15 minutes) that the client sends in the Authorization header with each API request. It is stateless — the server verifies its signature without a DB lookup. A refresh token is a long-lived opaque token (typically 7-30 days) stored as an HttpOnly cookie. It is used only to obtain a new access token when the old one expires. The client never sends the refresh token in API calls. Storing the refresh token in the DB allows revocation on logout or security events, while keeping access token verification fast and stateless.

What is the PKCE flow in OAuth2 and why was it introduced?:::PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) is an extension to the OAuth2 authorization code flow designed for public clients (mobile apps, SPAs) that cannot safely store a client_secret. The client generates a random code_verifier, hashes it to get the code_challenge, and sends the challenge when initiating the auth flow. The auth server stores it. When exchanging the code for tokens, the client sends the original code_verifier. The server hashes it and compares to the stored challenge — if they match, only the original client can redeem the code, preventing authorization code interception attacks.

What vulnerabilities should you defend against in a JWT implementation?:::Key vulnerabilities: (1) Algorithm confusion — always explicitly specify the expected algorithm in jwt.verify(); never accept 'none'. (2) Secret exposure — use long random secrets (256-bit minimum); never embed in client-side code. (3) Missing expiry — always set expiresIn. (4) Sensitive data in payload — JWT payload is base64-encoded, not encrypted; do not put passwords or PII in the payload. (5) HttpOnly cookies for refresh tokens — storing tokens in localStorage exposes them to XSS; HttpOnly cookies are not accessible to JavaScript.
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement a complete JWT auth flow with refresh token rotation, explain the security properties of bcrypt, and configure Passport.js strategies for local and OAuth2 authentication.

sessions.title: Database Integration — Prisma, Sequelize, Mongoose, Connection Pools
objectives: Define a Prisma schema, run migrations, and perform CRUD with relations and transactions
Configure Sequelize models with validations and associations
Connect to MongoDB with Mongoose and use virtuals, methods, and populate
Configure connection pools correctly for production workloads

activities.description: Set up a Prisma project with a User → Post → Comment schema. Implement paginated post listing with author included, create a post with a transaction that also creates an initial comment, and write a raw SQL query to find the top 5 most prolific authors.
Observe the N+1 problem by fetching 20 users and querying posts for each in a loop. Log all SQL queries. Then fix it with a single include/populate call and compare query counts.

reviewQuestions: What is the N+1 query problem and how do you fix it in Prisma?:::The N+1 problem: you fetch N records with one query, then for each record you execute one more query to fetch related data — N+1 total queries. For 100 users, fetching their posts causes 101 queries. In Prisma, fix it with include: { posts: true } in findMany. Prisma batches the related data fetch into a single additional query (using IN clause) instead of N separate queries. The total becomes 2 queries regardless of N. Always use include/select in Prisma to fetch relations rather than accessing them lazily.

Why is it important to create only one database connection pool instance per application?:::Each pool instance maintains its own set of connections to the database. Creating multiple pools wastes database connection slots (databases have limits, e.g., PostgreSQL default is 100 max_connections). If each incoming HTTP request creates its own pool, you quickly exhaust the database's connection limit under load. The correct pattern is to create one pool at startup (module singleton) and share it across all requests. In Node.js, module caching ensures require() of the same module always returns the same instance.

What is the difference between Prisma's findUnique and findFirst?:::findUnique can only filter on unique-constrained fields (primary key or @unique columns) and returns null if not found. It is the fastest lookup and communicates intent clearly — you expect at most one result. findFirst can filter on any combination of fields, applies orderBy and skip, and returns the first matching record or null. findFirst is appropriate when the uniqueness is business logic, not enforced by a DB constraint. Always prefer findUnique when querying by ID or other unique fields.

What happens if you forget to release a connection back to the pool?:::The connection is leaked — it remains checked out from the pool permanently. Over time, all pool slots are consumed by leaked connections and new requests block indefinitely waiting for a free connection (or fail with a connection timeout). The application becomes unresponsive. To prevent leaks, always release connections in a finally block, use the pool.query() shorthand (which manages checkout/release automatically), or use Prisma/Sequelize which handle connection lifecycle internally.
sessions.successCriteria: Can set up Prisma with a relational schema, perform CRUD with transactions, explain the N+1 problem and its fix, and configure a connection pool for production.

sessions.title: File Handling & Streams — Readable/Writable Streams, Buffers, Multer
objectives: Create and use Readable, Writable, and Transform streams
Implement a custom Transform stream for data processing
Handle file uploads with Multer including type validation and size limits
Stream file downloads to avoid loading entire files into memory

activities.description: Build a CSV processing pipeline: read a large CSV file as a stream, parse it with a custom Transform stream, filter rows matching a condition, and write the filtered rows to a new CSV — all without loading the full file into memory. Monitor process memory usage before and after.

Implement a file upload endpoint with Multer: accept images only, limit to 5 MB, rename files with a UUID, and serve them back as streaming downloads with proper Content-Type and Content-Disposition headers.

reviewQuestions: What is backpressure in Node.js streams and how does pipeline() handle it?:::Backpressure occurs when a Writable stream cannot consume data as fast as a Readable stream produces it. Without backpressure handling, the Readable keeps pushing data into memory, eventually causing an out-of-memory crash. The Writable stream's write() method returns false when its internal buffer is full — this signals the producer to pause. pipe() and pipeline() handle this automatically: when the destination returns false from write(), they call pause() on the source and resume() when the destination emits 'drain'. pipeline() also properly cleans up all streams if any stream emits an error, preventing resource leaks.

What is the difference between objectMode and the default binary mode in Transform streams?:::By default, Transform streams operate in binary mode: they accept and emit Buffers or strings. In objectMode, they can accept and emit any JavaScript value — arrays, objects, numbers. objectMode is used when you want to parse binary/text data and emit structured objects downstream (like a CSV parser emitting row objects). You cannot mix objectMode and binary mode streams in a pipeline without an intermediate transform that converts between the two formats.

What security risk does path traversal pose in file downloads, and how do you prevent it?:::Path traversal (directory traversal) occurs when a user provides a filename like ../../etc/passwd, causing the server to read files outside the intended directory. Prevention: (1) after constructing the full path with path.join(), check that it starts with the expected base directory using startsWith(); (2) never use req.params.filename directly in a path without sanitization; (3) serve files by an opaque ID stored in the database mapped to the real path, rather than exposing the filename directly.

How does Multer handle the difference between memoryStorage and diskStorage?:::With memoryStorage, Multer stores uploaded files in memory as Buffer objects (accessible via req.file.buffer). This is fast but dangerous for large files or concurrent uploads — multiple simultaneous large uploads can exhaust available RAM. With diskStorage, Multer streams the upload directly to disk as it arrives, using constant memory regardless of file size. diskStorage is preferred for production. Use memoryStorage only for small files that need immediate in-memory processing (e.g., image resizing before uploading to S3).
sessions.successCriteria: Can implement a streaming file processing pipeline with a custom Transform, handle file uploads securely with Multer, and explain backpressure and why pipeline() is preferred over .pipe().

sessions.title: WebSockets & Real-time — Socket.io, SSE, Pub/Sub
objectives: Implement a Socket.io server with namespaces, rooms, and JWT authentication middleware
Build an SSE endpoint for server-to-client streaming
Scale Socket.io across multiple processes using the Redis adapter
Choose between WebSockets and SSE based on communication pattern requirements

activities.description: Build a real-time chat application: Socket.io server with JWT auth, room join/leave, message broadcast, typing indicators, and connection tracking. Build a minimal HTML client that connects and sends/receives messages.
Implement an SSE endpoint for live notification updates. Use Node.js EventEmitter to dispatch events internally. Connect from a browser and verify events arrive without polling.

reviewQuestions: What is the WebSocket handshake and how does it differ from a regular HTTP request?:::A WebSocket connection starts with an HTTP Upgrade request. The client sends an HTTP GET with headers: 'Upgrade: websocket', 'Connection: Upgrade', and a 'Sec-WebSocket-Key' (base64 random bytes). The server responds with HTTP 101 Switching Protocols, echoing back a 'Sec-WebSocket-Accept' (SHA-1 hash of the key + a magic GUID). After the 101 response, the TCP connection is kept open and both parties switch to the WebSocket framing protocol — a lightweight binary framing format far smaller than HTTP headers. The connection is now bidirectional and persistent.

What are Socket.io rooms and how are they implemented internally?:::A Socket.io room is a named group that sockets can join. When a socket joins a room, Socket.io adds the socket's ID to an in-memory set associated with that room name. When you emit to a room (io.to('room-123').emit()), Socket.io iterates the set of socket IDs in that room and sends the event to each. With the Redis adapter, the room membership set is synchronized across processes via Redis. Under the hood, rooms are just JavaScript Maps — there is no database or persistent storage by default.

When would you choose SSE over WebSockets?:::Choose SSE when: (1) communication is only server-to-client (notifications, live feeds, order tracking); (2) you want to minimize infrastructure complexity — SSE runs over plain HTTP/1.1, works through all proxies and load balancers without special configuration; (3) you need automatic reconnection built into the browser (the EventSource API reconnects automatically). Choose WebSockets when: you need bidirectional real-time communication (chat, collaborative editing, games); or when sub-100ms latency is required since WebSocket frames have lower overhead than SSE's text-based framing.

Why do you need sticky sessions (or a Redis adapter) when running multiple Socket.io instances behind a load balancer?:::Socket.io's session state (room memberships, connection metadata) lives in the Node.js process's memory. Without sticky sessions, a client might connect to Process A, join a room, then disconnect and reconnect to Process B — which has no record of that room membership, causing event delivery failures. Sticky sessions route all connections from the same client to the same process (via cookie or IP hash). The Redis adapter is the better solution: it stores room state in Redis so all processes share the same view, enabling horizontal scaling without sticky session limitations.
sessions.successCriteria: Can build a Socket.io chat server with rooms and JWT auth, implement SSE for server-to-client streaming, and explain how Redis pub/sub enables Socket.io to scale across multiple Node.js processes.

sessions.title: Testing Node Apps — Jest, Supertest, Mocking, Integration Tests
objectives: Write unit tests for pure functions with edge cases and error conditions
Mock module dependencies with jest.mock() to isolate business logic
Write integration tests for Express routes using Supertest against a real test database
Distinguish between mocks, stubs, and spies and use each appropriately

activities.description: Write a full unit test suite for a UserService that creates users, sends emails, and throws on duplicate emails. Mock the database and email service. Aim for 100% line coverage on the UserService.

Write integration tests for a CRUD resource: test create, read (200 and 404), update, and delete. Use a dedicated test database, clean state in beforeAll, and test auth requirements for protected routes.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between unit tests and integration tests in a Node.js context?:::Unit tests test a single function or module in complete isolation. All external dependencies (databases, HTTP clients, file system) are mocked. They are fast (<1ms each), deterministic, and run without any external services. Integration tests test multiple components working together — typically an HTTP route handler, its middleware, the database query, and the response. They require a real (test) database, are slower (10-100ms each), and verify that the components integrate correctly. Unit tests catch logic bugs; integration tests catch wiring bugs and missing validations.

What does jest.mock() do and when should you use it?:::jest.mock('module-path') replaces all exports of that module with jest.fn() mock functions for the duration of the test file. It hoists the mock declaration to the top of the file before any imports. Use it when: (1) the real implementation has side effects (sends emails, charges credit cards, writes to DB); (2) the dependency is slow (database, network call); (3) you want to test error handling for scenarios that are hard to reproduce with the real dependency (DB connection failure, API timeout). Avoid mocking modules that are the subject of the test — that defeats the purpose.

Why should integration tests use a separate test database rather than the production or development database?:::Tests must be able to reset database state (TRUNCATE, DELETE) before each test or test suite to ensure isolation and repeatability. Doing this on a development database destroys your local data; doing it on production is catastrophic. A separate test database (pointed to by a TEST_DATABASE_URL env var) can be freely truncated, seeded, and dropped. Many teams use Docker Compose to spin up a fresh database container for CI runs, then destroy it after tests pass.

What does Supertest do that a manual HTTP client like axios does not?:::Supertest binds the Express app to an ephemeral port (or uses an in-process connection), sends real HTTP requests through the full Express middleware stack, and provides assertion helpers (expect(200), expect('Content-Type', /json/)). The key difference: Supertest does not require the server to be running as a separate process — it starts and stops the HTTP server internally per test run. This makes tests self-contained, faster, and reliable in CI environments without port conflicts.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write unit tests with full mock isolation and integration tests with Supertest against a real test database, explain the difference between mocks/stubs/spies, and achieve meaningful code coverage.

sessions.title: Security — Helmet, Rate Limiting, CORS, SQL Injection, XSS Prevention
objectives: Configure Helmet to set security headers including CSP, HSTS, and X-Frame-Options
Implement rate limiting per user and per IP using express-rate-limit with Redis
Configure CORS with a whitelist of allowed origins and credentials support
Prevent SQL injection with parameterized queries and XSS with sanitization

activities.description: Audit an existing Express API for security vulnerabilities: missing security headers, over-permissive CORS, rate limiting gaps, and unsanitized inputs. Fix each issue and document the attack vector each fix prevents.
Demonstrate a SQL injection attack on a vulnerable endpoint using your test database. Then fix it with parameterized queries and verify the same input is now harmless.

reviewQuestions: What does Content-Security-Policy (CSP) do and why is it important for a REST API?:::CSP is an HTTP response header that restricts which sources the browser can load resources (scripts, styles, images, fonts) from. For a REST API that returns JSON, CSP is still important if the API has any browser-facing pages (documentation, OAuth redirect pages, error pages). More critically, CSP protects any frontend applications consuming the API. A CSP that disallows inline scripts and limits script sources to 'self' means that even if an XSS vulnerability injects a script tag, the browser refuses to execute it — providing defense-in-depth.

What is prototype pollution and how can it affect a Node.js application?:::JavaScript objects have a prototype chain. If attacker-controlled data contains __proto__ or constructor.prototype keys, and your code merges that data into an existing object (e.g., Object.assign({}, req.body)), the attacker can modify Object.prototype — the root prototype shared by all objects. This can add properties to every object in the application, override methods, bypass security checks (e.g., isAdmin always returning true), or crash the application. Prevention: use schema validation (zod/Joi) that strips unknown keys, avoid Object.assign with untrusted input, and use npm audit to detect vulnerable versions of libraries that merge user input.

What is the Same-Origin Policy and what problem does CORS solve?:::The Same-Origin Policy (SOP) is a browser security mechanism that prevents JavaScript on page A (e.g., evil.com) from making requests to page B's domain (e.g., bank.com) and reading the response. CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is the mechanism by which a server explicitly opts in to allowing cross-origin requests from specific trusted origins. Without CORS headers, the browser blocks the response even if the server sent it. CORS is a browser enforcement mechanism — it does not protect server-to-server requests (curl, Postman, Node.js fetch), which is why it is not a substitute for server-side authentication.

How does rate limiting protect against brute-force attacks, and what information should it not leak?:::Rate limiting caps the number of requests a client can make in a time window, making brute-force password or API key guessing impractical (e.g., 10 login attempts per hour allows ~87,600 guesses per year — bcrypt with 12 rounds brings this to ~3 guesses/hour in wall time). Critical: rate limit responses should return generic error messages without specifying whether the account exists. 'Invalid email or password' prevents user enumeration; 'Account not found' leaks that the email exists. Rate limit headers (RateLimit-Remaining, Retry-After) help legitimate clients but should not reveal whether the block is per-IP or per-account.
sessions.successCriteria: Can configure Helmet, rate limiting, and CORS correctly, demonstrate a parameterized query preventing SQL injection, and explain the attack vectors for XSS, CSRF, and prototype pollution.

sessions.title: Deployment — PM2, Docker, Cluster Mode, Monitoring, Logging
objectives: Configure PM2 in cluster mode with ecosystem.config.js
Write a multi-stage Dockerfile that produces a minimal production image as a non-root user
Implement graceful shutdown that drains in-flight requests before closing connections
Add structured JSON logging and health check endpoints for Kubernetes readiness/liveness probes

activities.description: Containerize an Express API with a multi-stage Dockerfile. Add a HEALTHCHECK instruction. Run with Docker Compose alongside a PostgreSQL container. Verify the health check passes and the API can query the database.

Implement graceful shutdown: start the server, send 1000 in-flight requests, then send SIGTERM. Verify that all in-flight requests complete before the process exits, and that new requests after SIGTERM receive 503.

reviewQuestions: What is the difference between SIGTERM and SIGKILL and why does it matter for Node.js?:::SIGTERM is a graceful shutdown signal — the process receives it and can run cleanup code (finish in-flight requests, close DB connections, flush log buffers) before exiting. Kubernetes sends SIGTERM when a pod is terminated, waits for the grace period (default 30s), then sends SIGKILL. SIGKILL immediately terminates the process — no cleanup code runs. If a Node.js server does not handle SIGTERM, Kubernetes will SIGKILL it after the grace period, potentially dropping in-flight requests and leaving DB connections unclosed. Always handle SIGTERM with a graceful shutdown routine.

What is the purpose of a multi-stage Docker build for a Node.js application?:::A multi-stage build uses multiple FROM instructions in one Dockerfile. Earlier stages do heavy work (install all dependencies including devDependencies, compile TypeScript). The final stage copies only what is needed for production (compiled JS, production node_modules) into a clean image. Result: the production image is much smaller (no devDependencies, no compiler tools, no source files), reducing attack surface, pull times, and registry storage. A typical Node.js app goes from ~1 GB (with all dev tools) to ~150-200 MB in production image.

What is the difference between liveness and readiness health check probes in Kubernetes?:::A liveness probe checks if the process is alive and should keep running — if it fails, Kubernetes restarts the container. It should only check if the application's main loop is functioning (e.g., can respond to HTTP). A readiness probe checks if the process is ready to serve traffic — if it fails, Kubernetes removes the pod from the load balancer's endpoints but does not restart it. Readiness fails during startup (before the DB connection is established) and during graceful shutdown (after SIGTERM is received). This combination prevents traffic from reaching unready pods while avoiding unnecessary restarts.

Why should Node.js log to stdout/stderr in JSON format rather than to files?:::In containerized environments (Docker, Kubernetes), containers are ephemeral — their file system is not persistent. Logs written to files inside the container are lost when the container restarts. Logging to stdout/stderr allows the container runtime (Docker, Kubernetes) to capture all logs and forward them to a centralized log aggregator (CloudWatch, Datadog, ELK stack). JSON format makes logs machine-parseable — log aggregators can index fields (userId, requestId, statusCode) for filtering, alerting, and dashboards. Human-readable log formats require parsing with regex, which is fragile.
sessions.successCriteria: Can write a production Dockerfile with multi-stage builds and a non-root user, configure PM2 in cluster mode, implement graceful shutdown with SIGTERM handling, and explain the difference between liveness and readiness probes.

quizBank.question: What is the correct order of Node.js event loop phases?
options: A) timers → poll → check → pending callbacks
B) poll → timers → check → close callbacks
C) timers → pending callbacks → idle/prepare → poll → check → close callbacks
D) check → timers → poll → close callbacks
quizBank.explanation: The Node.js event loop runs in a fixed order: timers (setTimeout/setInterval) → pending callbacks (deferred I/O errors) → idle/prepare (internal) → poll (I/O callbacks, blocking wait) → check (setImmediate) → close callbacks. process.nextTick() and Promise microtasks run between every phase transition.

quizBank.question: What does non-blocking I/O mean in Node.js?
options: A) I/O operations run on a separate CPU core
B) I/O operations are delegated to the OS/libuv thread pool, and the event loop continues processing other callbacks while waiting
C) I/O operations are batched and executed in bulk
D) I/O operations are performed synchronously but in a background process
quizBank.explanation: Non-blocking I/O means Node.js delegates I/O (file reads, network calls) to libuv, which uses OS-level async syscalls or a thread pool. The event loop never stalls — it continues picking up other callbacks while waiting for I/O to complete. This enables high concurrency on a single thread.

quizBank.question: In Express.js, what must middleware do to pass control to the next middleware in the stack?
options: A) return true
B) call res.continue()
C) call next()
D) call req.next()
quizBank.explanation: Express middleware functions receive (req, res, next). To pass control downstream, middleware must call next(). Calling next(err) skips to error-handling middleware. If neither next() nor a response method (res.send, res.json) is called, the request hangs. This is a common source of bugs — always call one or the other.

quizBank.question: Which HTTP method and status code should a REST API use when a resource is successfully created?
options: A) POST with 200 OK
B) PUT with 201 Created
C) POST with 201 Created
D) POST with 204 No Content
quizBank.explanation: REST convention: POST to a collection endpoint creates a resource and returns 201 Created. The response body typically includes the created resource, and the Location header optionally points to the new resource URL. 200 OK is for successful GETs/updates. 204 No Content is for successful DELETE or updates with no response body.

quizBank.question: In JWT authentication, where should the JWT secret key be stored in a Node.js application?
options: A) Hardcoded in the source file for reliability
B) In a config.json file committed to the repository
C) In an environment variable loaded via .env (never committed)
D) In the JWT payload so it can be verified client-side
quizBank.explanation: JWT secrets must be stored in environment variables (e.g., process.env.JWT_SECRET), loaded via .env files that are gitignored. Hardcoding secrets or committing them to version control is a critical security vulnerability. The secret must never appear in the JWT payload — the payload is base64-encoded and readable by anyone.

quizBank.question: What is the key advantage of Node.js Readable Streams over reading an entire file with fs.readFile()?
options: A) Streams are faster because they use multiple threads
B) Streams process data in chunks, keeping memory usage constant regardless of file size
C) Streams automatically compress data during transfer
D) Streams skip the event loop for better performance
quizBank.explanation: fs.readFile() loads the entire file into memory before processing — a 4 GB file uses 4 GB of RAM. Readable Streams process data in configurable chunks (default 64 KB), keeping memory usage nearly constant. This is why stream piping is essential for file uploads, video serving, and large data exports: the server stays responsive and memory-efficient.

quizBank.question: What is the primary difference between WebSockets and Server-Sent Events (SSE)?
options: A) WebSockets are encrypted; SSE is not
B) SSE supports binary data; WebSockets do not
C) WebSockets are full-duplex (bidirectional); SSE is unidirectional (server to client only)
D) SSE requires a special server; WebSockets work over standard HTTP
quizBank.explanation: WebSockets establish a persistent full-duplex TCP connection — both client and server can send messages at any time. SSE uses a long-lived HTTP connection for server-to-client push only; the client cannot send data over the same channel. Use WebSockets for chat/gaming/collaboration. Use SSE for dashboards, notifications, and log streaming where the client only listens.

quizBank.question: Which security header prevents Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) attacks by controlling which resources a browser may load?
options: A) X-Frame-Options
B) Strict-Transport-Security
C) Content-Security-Policy
D) X-Content-Type-Options
quizBank.explanation: Content-Security-Policy (CSP) instructs the browser to only load scripts, styles, and other resources from explicitly whitelisted sources. This prevents XSS by blocking inline scripts and external untrusted scripts. In Node.js/Express, the helmet package sets CSP and other security headers. X-Frame-Options prevents clickjacking, HSTS enforces HTTPS, and X-Content-Type-Options prevents MIME sniffing.

quizBank.question: What does PM2 cluster mode achieve compared to running a single Node.js process?
options: A) It enables GPU acceleration for compute-heavy tasks
B) It spawns multiple worker processes matching CPU core count, sharing the same port via the master process
C) It distributes requests to multiple servers over the network
D) It compiles JavaScript to native code for better performance
quizBank.explanation: Node.js is single-threaded. PM2 cluster mode uses Node's built-in cluster module to fork multiple child processes (typically one per CPU core). The PM2 master process load-balances incoming connections across workers using round-robin. This multiplies throughput for CPU-bound or high-concurrency workloads. pm2 start app.js -i max automatically uses all available cores.

quizBank.question: What is the correct way to handle uncaught Promise rejections in a production Node.js application?
options: A) Ignore them — Node.js will retry the operation automatically
B) Use process.on('unhandledRejection') to log and gracefully terminate, while fixing root causes with proper async/await try-catch
C) Wrap all code in a global try-catch block
D) Use domains (deprecated) to catch all async errors
quizBank.explanation: Unhandled Promise rejections indicate programming errors. Best practice: (1) Use try-catch in every async function to handle expected errors. (2) Register process.on('unhandledRejection', (reason) => { logger.error(reason); process.exit(1); }) as a safety net — an unhandled rejection means the app is in an unknown state. Since Node.js 15+, unhandled rejections crash the process by default. Domains are deprecated and should not be used.
